whiskas-ocean-fish-adult.title: Ocean Fish, Adult 1+

verdict.label: Okay for now
verdict.summary: Safe enough for a healthy adult cat, but the label hides important details like carbs, taurine, and mineral balance.

worry.label: Medium concern
worry.note: No need to panic. The main issue is that this brand doesn't reveal enough for you to confidently judge long-term quality.

whiskas-ocean-fish-adult.action: Finish the current pack if your cat is healthy. Don't bulk buy. Before your next refill, compare options with named meat sources and taurine disclosure.

reasons.a: 33% dry matter protein — above the 26% minimum for adult cats.

reasons.q: More carbs than your cat naturally needs
reasons.a: ~39% estimated. Cats in the wild eat around 5%. This is grain padding.

reasons.q: Heart & eye nutrient not clearly listed
reasons.a: Taurine is essential for cats. This brand doesn't disclose how much is present.

whiskas-ocean-fish-adult.parentTake: I wouldn't throw this away immediately. But I also wouldn't make it the only daily food long-term. I'd finish the pack, then compare options with clearer meat sources, taurine disclosure, and lower estimated carbs.

f1.label: Checks out
f1.tip: Protein at 33% dry matter exceeds the 26% AAFCO minimum.

f2.label: Worth a sniff
f2.tip: Wild prey is ~5% carbs. 39% means grain padding.

f3.label: Gone missing
f3.tip: Essential amino acid. Deficiency causes blindness.

whiskas-ocean-fish-adult.ingRest: then: meat & by-products · oils · minerals · vitamins. Meat source not named.
whiskas-ocean-fish-adult.nose: Meets minimums. Cereal-first, won't name the meat, and declines to publish taurine. Adequate as a base — but at ₹60/100g, options exist with named protein and full disclosure.

royal-canin-indoor-adult.title: Indoor Adult

verdict.label: Good enough
verdict.summary: One of the more transparent options in India. Names its protein, discloses taurine. The premium price buys accountability, not just marketing.

worry.label: Low concern
worry.note: Nothing here screams concern. This is one of the more transparent options available in India.

royal-canin-indoor-adult.action: Keep feeding this with reasonable confidence. The premium price buys transparency, not just ingredients. Still worth supplementing with wet food for hydration.

reasons.a: 36% dry matter — well above the minimum. Good for indoor adults.
~33% estimated. Better than most Indian kibble, but higher than what cats naturally eat.
Dehydrated poultry protein first. Taurine at 0.14% — above the 0.10% minimum. That's rare in India.

royal-canin-indoor-adult.parentTake: I'd feel reasonably confident feeding this daily. It's not perfect — carbs are still higher than I'd like — but it's transparent, names its protein, and discloses taurine. That puts it ahead of most options in India.

f1.label: Checks out
f1.tip: 36% dry matter protein is solid for indoor adults.

f2.label: Worth a sniff
f2.tip: Still higher than ideal but better than most Indian kibble.

f3.label: Checks out
f3.tip: Disclosed at 0.14%. Meets the AAFCO minimum of 0.10%.

royal-canin-indoor-adult.ingRest: then: rice · wheat · animal fats · vegetable fibres. Named protein — good.
royal-canin-indoor-adult.nose: Above average. Named protein first, discloses taurine. Carbs still high for indoor cats. The premium price buys transparency, not just ingredients.

me-o-tuna-adult.title: Tuna, Adult

verdict.label: Okay for now
verdict.summary: Similar nutrition profile to Whiskas at nearly half the price. Same transparency gaps, same tradeoffs.

worry.label: Medium concern
worry.note: Not dangerous, but the brand tells you very little. Same disclosure gaps as Whiskas at a lower price.

me-o-tuna-adult.action: If budget is the main constraint, this does the job short-term. Don't assume cheaper means worse — compare what each brand actually discloses before your next purchase.

reasons.a: 33% dry matter — same as Whiskas, at nearly half the price.
~38% estimated. Standard for budget kibble in India, but not great for daily feeding.

reasons.q: Same transparency gaps as the big brands
reasons.a: No taurine, no ash, no mineral balance disclosed. Generic fish source — not named.

me-o-tuna-adult.parentTake: I'd use this if budget mattered most. It's essentially Whiskas at a lower price point — same strengths, same gaps. I wouldn't feel great about it long-term, but I also wouldn't panic.

f1.label: Checks out
f1.tip: Matches Whiskas at nearly half the price.

f2.label: Worth a sniff
f2.tip: High carbs, standard for budget kibble in India.

f3.label: Gone missing
f3.tip: Same gap as Whiskas. Essential, unpublished.

me-o-tuna-adult.ingRest: then: fish & fish by-products · soybean meal · oils · vitamins. Generic fish, not named.
me-o-tuna-adult.nose: Similar to Whiskas at nearly half the price. Same cereal-first problem, same disclosure gaps. Budget-friendly — but you get what you get.

drools-ocean-fish-adult.title: Ocean Fish, Adult

verdict.label: Not transparent enough
verdict.summary: The cheapest option available. Meets bare minimums, but reveals almost nothing beyond what regulations force.

worry.label: Higher concern
worry.note: This is the bare minimum. Cereal-heavy, minimal protein, and the least transparent label in this group.

drools-ocean-fish-adult.action: Use only if budget is very tight. Supplement with wet food when you can. This should be a stepping stone, not a permanent choice. Plan to upgrade when budget allows.

reasons.a: 32% dry matter — just above the 26% minimum. Adequate, nothing more.
~40% estimated. Heavy on cereals and fillers. Likely ingredient splitting.
No taurine, no ash, no mineral balance, generic meat source. Almost nothing disclosed beyond what's legally required.

drools-ocean-fish-adult.parentTake: I'd treat this as a temporary solution, not a permanent choice. At ₹22/100g it's the cheapest option, but you can feel the corners being cut. I'd supplement with wet food and plan to upgrade when budget allows.

f1.label: Checks out
f1.tip: Just above AAFCO minimum. Adequate, nothing more.

f2.label: Worth a sniff
f2.tip: 40% carbs — highest in this group. Heavy on fillers.

f3.label: Gone missing
f3.tip: Not disclosed. Common in Indian budget brands.

drools-ocean-fish-adult.ingRest: then: fish meal · soybean meal · oils · vitamins. Ingredient splitting on cereals.
drools-ocean-fish-adult.nose: The cheapest option. Meets minimums, barely. Cereal-heavy with likely ingredient splitting. For tight budgets only — supplement with wet food if you can.

sheba-tuna-in-jelly.title: Tuna in Jelly

verdict.label: Strong choice
verdict.tag: Close to what cats naturally eat
verdict.summary: Named protein, low carbs, high moisture. This is what a cat's body actually expects from food.

worry.label: Low concern
worry.note: This is one of the better options available. Your cat's body was designed for food like this.

sheba-tuna-in-jelly.action: Keep feeding this. It doesn't replace dry food for dental health, but as a primary protein source, this is excellent. Pair with a transparent dry food for a strong daily rotation.

reasons.a: 52% dry matter — close to what cats eat in the wild. Among the highest available.
~8% estimated. Wet food naturally runs lower, and this is excellent.
Tuna first. You know what your cat is eating. That transparency matters.

sheba-tuna-in-jelly.parentTake: This would be a staple in my rotation. Named tuna, low carbs, 80% moisture — it's close to what cats evolved to eat. I'd pair it with a transparent dry food for dental health and call it a solid diet.

f1.label: Checks out
f1.tip: 52% dry-matter protein. Close to prey-matched levels.

f2.label: Checks out
f2.tip: 8% carbs — excellent. Wet food naturally runs lower.

f3.label: Checks out
f3.tip: 80% moisture. Cats evolved to get water from food.

sheba-tuna-in-jelly.ingRest: then: water · tapioca starch · gelling agents. Named protein. Short list.
sheba-tuna-in-jelly.nose: Strong. Named protein first, low carbs, high moisture. Doesn't replace dry food for dental health — but as the primary protein source, this is what a cat's body actually expects.
